items.prompt: While scanning an array, the current prefix sum is s. To count subarrays ending at the current index whose sum is exactly k, which prior prefix value must be matched?
constraints: Prefix sums are taken before and through the current position under one consistent convention.
The array may contain negative values.

options.text: A prior prefix equal to s - k.
A prior prefix equal to s + k.
Any prior prefix equal to k, regardless of s.
Only a prior prefix equal to the current s.

feedback.reason: If currentPrefix - priorPrefix = k, then priorPrefix must equal currentPrefix - k.
feedback.details: A subarray sum is the difference between two prefix states. Rearranging s - p = k gives p = s - k, so a frequency map can report how many valid starts exist for the current end. The rule does not require sorted or nonnegative values; it relies only on algebraic cancellation and on querying prior state.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.s_plus_k: This would make s - priorPrefix = -k, not k. The sign follows directly from rearranging the range-difference equation.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.k_only: The required prior state changes with the current prefix s. A fixed lookup of k ignores where the current scan has accumulated to.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.current_s: Matching s counts zero-sum subarrays, because s - s = 0. It is correct only when k = 0.

items.prompt: To count all subarrays with sum k, what should the hash map store for each prefix value?
constraints: Different start boundaries may have the same prefix value.
The output is a count, not one example subarray.

options.text: The number of prior boundaries that produced that prefix value.
Only the most recent index where the prefix value occurred.
The sorted list of input values seen so far.
The length of the current moving window.

feedback.reason: Every prior occurrence of s - k defines a distinct valid start boundary, so multiplicity is part of the answer.
feedback.details: When the current prefix is s, the answer increases by frequency[s - k]. Storing only one index can find one candidate but loses other starts with the same cumulative value. The map represents historical boundary multiplicity, not a moving window or a sorted value summary.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.last_index_only: A last-index map is sufficient for some existence or longest-length variants, but it undercounts when multiple prior boundaries have the required value.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_values: Sorting values loses the prefix-by-position relation. The needed key is a cumulative boundary value, and the needed payload is its count.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.current_window_length: Negative values can make valid starts non-monotonic, so one current window does not enumerate all subarrays ending here.

items.prompt: Order the operations for counting subarrays with sum k during one left-to-right prefix-hash scan.
constraints: The frequency map contains only prefix boundaries that occur before the current boundary when the lookup happens.
Score is based on preserved adjacent relations.

elements.text: Initialize frequency[0] = 1 for the empty prefix.
Add the current array value to the running prefix s.
Read frequency[s - k] and add it to the answer.
Advance to the next array position.

feedback.reason: The lookup must see prior boundaries but not count the current boundary as its own earlier start.
feedback.details: Seeding zero represents the boundary before index 0. After extending s, the scan counts prior p = s - k, then records the current boundary for future ends. Recording first is wrong when k = 0 because it can count an empty current-to-current interval; the required relation is lookup-before-insert for the current prefix.

items.prompt: Why must frequency[0] start at 1 before scanning when counting subarrays with sum k?
constraints: The running prefix before reading any element is 0.

options.text: It represents the one boundary before index 0, allowing a current prefix equal to k to count a subarray starting at 0.
It prevents the running prefix from becoming negative.
It makes hash-map iteration sorted by key.
It lets the algorithm run without storing any other prefix values.

feedback.reason: The empty prefix is a real start boundary with cumulative value zero.
feedback.details: If s = k at index r, then the required prior prefix is s - k = 0. The seeded occurrence corresponds to the boundary before A[0], so the subarray [0, r] is counted uniformly. Omitting the seed forces a special case and misses these ranges; adding it does not control signs, ordering, or storage size.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.avoid_negative_prefixes: The running sum may legitimately be negative. The seed exists for boundary completeness, not monotonicity.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_hash_keys: A hash map does not become sorted because one key is initialized. Lookup order is irrelevant to correctness.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.remove_extra_space: The algorithm still stores frequencies for potentially O(n) distinct prefix values. One seed does not eliminate auxiliary state.

items.prompt: Why is prefix sum plus hash frequency suitable for counting exact-sum subarrays when A may contain negative values?
constraints: All subarrays with sum k must be counted.
No sorting or reordering is permitted.

options.text: It matches the required prior cumulative value even when the running sum rises and falls, so no monotonic shrink rule is needed.
The hash map converts every negative value into a positive value.
It is simply a moving window stored in a hash table.
It first sorts the array so all negative values appear before positive values.

feedback.reason: The lookup equation works over non-monotonic cumulative history; it does not depend on a one-direction window repair rule.
feedback.details: With negative values, increasing the right boundary can increase or decrease the sum, so a simple “shrink while too large” window can miss solutions. Prefix-hash matching asks only whether prior s - k states exist, preserving every legal start boundary. It remains a prefix-history method, not a window hidden inside a map.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.negatives_become_positive: Hashing does not change values. Correctness comes from exact key equality on cumulative sums, including negative keys.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.window_and_prefix_same: A window maintains one current interval and moves its boundaries. Prefix-hash state retains many prior boundaries simultaneously.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_then_hash: Sorting destroys subarray contiguity and is unnecessary. The scan must follow original order.

items.prompt: A developer sorts A before running the prefix-hash algorithm for subarray sum k. Why is that invalid?
constraints: A subarray means a contiguous segment of the original sequence.

options.text: Sorting changes which values are adjacent, so prefix differences describe subarrays of a different sequence.
Hash maps require insertion keys in descending order.
The running prefix must be increasing, and sorting cannot guarantee that with duplicates.
Sorting prevents storing a frequency for the same key twice.

feedback.reason: The prefix-hash equation is positional; reordering changes the set of contiguous segments being counted.
feedback.details: The algorithm works on the original scan because each pair of prefix boundaries identifies one original subarray. Sorting creates new adjacencies and therefore a different family of boundary pairs. Hash keys may arrive in any order, and prefix sums need not increase.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.hash_requires_order: Hash lookup has no sorted insertion requirement. Arbitrary cumulative values are valid keys.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_must_increase: Prefixes can rise, fall, or repeat. The matching equation uses equality, not monotonicity.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.frequency_lost: A frequency map explicitly supports repeated keys. Sorting does not prevent repetition; it changes the sequence semantics.

items.prompt: Select the expected time and worst-case extra space for one prefix-hash scan that counts subarrays with sum k.
constraints: Hash lookup and update are expected O(1).
Up to n + 1 distinct prefix sums may occur.
The input array is not counted as extra space.

feedback.reason: The scan performs expected constant hash work per element and may retain a distinct frequency entry for every prefix boundary.
feedback.details: There are n iterations, each with one required-prior lookup and one current-prefix update, giving expected O(n) time. In the worst case all n + 1 prefix values are distinct, so the map uses O(n) extra space. The rolling sum itself is constant state, but the historical frequency map is the essential auxiliary memory.

items.prompt: Which problem transfers the required-prior-prefix idea by storing a normalized prefix state instead of the raw sum?
constraints: Subarrays are contiguous in the original order.
All qualifying subarrays must be counted.

options.text: Count subarrays whose sum is divisible by m by matching equal normalized prefix remainders.
Answer range minima by subtracting two prefix remainders.
Maintain the sorted order of values by hashing each prefix sum.
Find the maximum fixed-size window by matching equal prefix frequencies only.

feedback.reason: Two prefixes with the same remainder modulo m differ by a multiple of m, so equality of normalized states is the match condition.
feedback.details: For current remainder r, every prior boundary with the same normalized remainder defines a subarray sum divisible by m. The map still stores frequencies, and negative language remainders may need normalization into [0, m). Range minimum has no inverse, while a fixed-size maximum is governed by window movement rather than frequency matching.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.range_minimum: Equal or subtractable remainders do not encode minima. Minimum is not derived from a pair of scalar cumulative states.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.online_sorting: Hashing prefix sums does not maintain value order. This is neither a sorting contract nor a contiguous-subarray condition.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.fixed_window_max: A fixed-size maximum can be maintained by adding the entering value and removing the leaving value. Prefix-frequency equality is not the decision rule.
